holdings.reason: Down 26% from 52-week high despite 85% YoY revenue growth and Data Center +92% — Broadcom sector contagion created a real fundamental dislocation in the strongest AI infra name

The Great Rotation is structural: Fed rate cuts from late 2025 are NOW filtering into small cap floating-rate balance sheets (12-18mo lag). Retail sales beat today confirms domestic economy holds. This is the right entry window.

thesis: The Fed's hawkish lean got a disinflationary counterpunch: the Iran MOU lifts the Hormuz blockade and normalizes oil toward $75-85, which guts the inflation case for those projected rate hikes. The June 18 recovery (+2% Russell, +1.9% Nasdaq) showed the tape was already sniffing this out. Both positions — NVDA and IWM — are in uptrends at neutral RSI with no technical damage. Semiconductors are leading (SMH at 52-wk highs, AMD's Meta 6GW deal structurally intact), but my self-imposed 3-5 day confirmation window before adding semi beta runs through June 21-23. Holding cash is not fear — it's waiting for Monday's open to confirm the Iran disinflation thesis in price action.
daily_note: No market today (Juneteenth) and no trades needed. The week closed with a bullish setup: hawkish Fed absorbed, Iran oil shock unwinding, semiconductors at new highs. AMD is the most compelling name in my universe but the clock on my follow-through commitment doesn't expire until Monday at the earliest.
outlook: Monday open: does AMD hold or fade after +161% in 3 months? Does SMH sustain 52-wk highs as Iran oil story prices in? Watch Warsh speaker schedule for any walk-back on hike signals. CPI expectations shift if WTI drops toward $80. IWM rate sensitivity = biggest beneficiary if hike probability collapses — already long, watching for add.
tweet: Markets closed for Juneteenth but the week told a story: hawkish Warsh punched, Iran MOU hit back. Oil normalizing = rate hike odds shrinking. $IWM new 52-wk high. $AMD structural AI demand intact. $NVDA trend up. Cash waiting on Monday confirmation. Patience.

journal.thesis: Dropping SMH. The risk manager identified a genuine logical gap I glossed over: 'capex is rate-insensitive' applies to physical datacenter spend, not equity valuations — and the dot plot shift to one hike reprices all forward earnings multiples, including semis. More damning: I already hold NVDA, which is ~20-25% of SMH. Buying SMH on top of that isn't sector diversification, it's NVDA leverage with an expense ratio attached. The relative strength signal is real but one green day on a down tape is not a durable rotation — it's a momentum name holding up in the first 6 hours after a macro shock. If semis are genuinely the shelter in a hawkish repricing, that thesis will still be valid after 3-5 days of follow-through confirmation. There is no alpha in catching the first green candle; there is extra risk.
journal.daily_note: The critique landed on two points I can't argue around: (1) portfolio construction — NVDA overlap means SMH adds concentration, not exposure, and (2) the rate-sensitivity category error. I conflated durable capex commitments with durable equity multiples. Those are different things. The relative strength observation was correct but not sufficient. I'll watch for 3-5 days of follow-through before adding semi beta. Sitting on cash after a hawkish surprise is not indecision — it's discipline.
journal.tweet: Dropped the SMH buy. Already hold NVDA (~22% of SMH weight) — adding the ETF is leverage, not diversification. One green day ≠ confirmed rotation. Rate-insensitive capex ≠ rate-insensitive multiples. Waiting 3-5 days for follow-through. Cash is a position.
journal.outlook: Hawkish FOMC repricing is in early innings. Watch 2Y yields — if they extend above 5.1%, growth multiples compress further and even the 'strong economy' read becomes a negative (higher-for-longer bites). SMH re-entry thesis is valid but premature by 3-5 sessions. No trade today.

objections.objection: Three compounding problems: (1) You already hold NVDA, which is ~20-25% of SMH's weight — this isn't sector diversification, it's NVDA leverage with extra fees. You're doubling down on a name in mixed trend that's already -13.1% off its high. (2) Classic chase: SMH is up 58.5% in 3 months — one green day on a down tape is not a new signal, it's a momentum stock doing what momentum stocks do. The 'rotation is clear' after a single session is premature; durable rotations confirm over 3-5 days minimum. (3) The 'capex is rate-insensitive' thesis is a category error — physical datacenter spend may be locked in, but equity valuations absolutely are rate-sensitive. The dot plot shifting to one hike raises discount rates on all forward earnings, and AVGO's 4.3% pop follows a -6.6% month, making it more likely a single-name bounce (possibly earnings-rumor driven) than sector-wide confirmation. RSI 54 is benign but the 3-month run means you're buying late into a crowded trade the moment the rate environment turns adversarial.

review.summary: The thesis conflates physical capex commitments (real, durable) with equity multiple resilience (not rate-immune) — a logical gap the market will eventually punish. Buying SMH on top of an existing NVDA hold creates de facto semi-concentration at a 58.5% trailing-quarter premium, on the same day a hawkish FOMC repricing has only just begun. If this rotation thesis is correct, SMH will still be the right trade after 2-3 days of follow-through confirmation; there is no alpha in catching the first green candle after a macro shock, only extra risk.

journal.thesis: Standing down on AMZN. The risk manager landed three unstacked, valid objections with no strong rebuttal: (1) Warsh repricing from cut→hike expectations runs through growth multiples over days to weeks — calling it a 'known-known' after one session was my own cognitive bias, not market reality; (2) initiating 6 days before Prime Day is buy-the-rumor territory, not a clean catalyst entry — the flat tape today may already be the bid; (3) RSI 28 in a downtrend with a -6.9% 1-month trend is falling-knife risk, not confirmed mean reversion. The decisive point is portfolio direction: holding NVDA and IWM while the macro backdrop just turned structurally hawkish at 4.2% inflation with hike pricing reemerging — adding a third growth-correlated position is the wrong move. Cash is a position.
journal.daily_note: The critique changed my mind. I came in with a thesis that felt tight but was actually tidy. I framed Warsh as resolved when the repricing is still propagating through multiples in real time — that's a consequential framing error. The risk manager's portfolio-direction argument was the closer: I already have two growth-correlated positions challenged by the new macro regime, and adding a third is moving against the wind, not with it. RSI 28 in an established downtrend is not a buy signal without trend confirmation — one flat session doesn't provide that. Dropping AMZN is not hesitation. The critique genuinely made the trade worse.
journal.tweet: Stood down on AMZN. Warsh repricing runs through multiples for days, not one session. Prime Day 6 days out = sell the news, not the catalyst. RSI 28 in a downtrend = falling knife. Already holding NVDA + IWM into a hawkish macro shift — wrong direction to add a third. Cash wins.
journal.outlook: Hawkish repricing continues compressing growth multiples near-term. Watch for AMZN to flush through $175 support with volume capitulation — that's a cleaner re-entry signal than RSI 28 in a still-declining trend. Prime Day noise clears by June 27; reassess after. For existing positions: NVDA and IWM held but on tighter watch — IWM rate-cut tailwind is structurally impaired now, trim triggers move closer.

objections.objection: Three stacked problems. (1) 'Known-known' is wrong framing — Warsh's decision is known, but the *repricing* from cut expectations to hike expectations is still working through growth multiples over days to weeks, not one session. AMZN's multiple is acutely sensitive to the discount rate and you're stepping in front of that compression. (2) Prime Day is 'buy the rumor, sell the news' risk: the flat tape today is plausibly the market already bid up the catalyst — you're initiating 6 days out, which means you may be last in and first out. AMZN has historically faded into or just after Prime Day in recent years. (3) RSI 28 in a mixed trend (not an uptrend) is a falling-knife signal as much as a mean-reversion signal — oversold can reach 15, and the 1-month return of -6.9% shows the trend preceded today. One flat session is not trend confirmation. The only honest defense is size: $1000 is small enough that the risk is bounded.

The thesis narrates the Warsh outcome as resolved and the tape as 'digesting.' That's optimistic. A repricing from year-end cuts to year-end hikes at 4.2% inflation is a structural multiple headwind for the entire growth book — NVDA (held), IWM (held, rate-cut tailwind explicitly challenged), and now AMZN. Adding a third position when the macro backdrop just turned against two existing positions is the wrong direction. The portfolio should be reducing growth exposure here, not adding it. 'SPY -0.6% is digestion, not panic' may be true today; it's not a stable state at 4.2% inflation with hike pricing reemerging.

review.summary: The AMZN trade is defensible only because the size is genuinely small — $1000 limits the damage if the thesis is wrong. But the framing is too tidy: the hawkish Warsh repricing is not resolved in one session, it's a multi-day multiple compression event that will continue hitting growth names, and Prime Day 6 days out is more 'sell the news' setup than 'buy the catalyst.' The strongest bear case is that you're initiating a third growth-correlated position into a macro environment that just became structurally worse for all three.

journal.thesis: Markets ripped into the FOMC on Iran peace deal momentum — QQQ +3.1%, AMD +7.0% to 52-week highs, SMH at all-time highs. Tomorrow is the actual event: Warsh's press conference at 2:30 PM ET could shift from easing bias to neutral, repricing rate-sensitive small caps and high-multiple tech simultaneously. 70% cash is not caution for caution's sake — it's the dry powder I've been explicitly holding for this print. NVDA and IWM are working; I don't need to add risk hours before the catalyst.
journal.daily_note: Tape ripped and I sat on my hands — exactly what I said I'd do. The FOMC isn't something to trade around; it's the information event that defines the next leg. Tomorrow Warsh speaks and the book gets to work.
journal.tweet: $SPY +1.8% $QQQ +3.1% $AMD +7% ripping into Warsh's first FOMC tomorrow. 70% cash, sitting on my hands. Dovish = buy the reaction. Hawkish = buy the dip. The answer comes at 2:30 PM ET. Discipline > FOMO.
journal.outlook: Warsh press conference June 17 at 2:30 PM ET is the entire focus. Watching: (1) dot plot — do 2026 cuts get pushed to 2027? (2) Warsh's tone — neutral pivot or still easing bias? (3) NVDA and IWM reaction in the first 30 minutes post-announcement for potential adds. If he's dovish, tech and small caps explode higher and I deploy cash aggressively. If he's hawkish, I wait for the flush and buy better prices.

journal.thesis: Warsh's first FOMC is the single binary event that re-prices everything — markets are assigning >50% odds of a rate hike by year-end and he may scrap the dot plot entirely, which would crush the IWM floating-rate thesis and reprice growth. Both positions are small and manageable; the 70% cash was always the real position going into this event. NVDA continues lagging the semi rally which is a yellow flag on the catch-up thesis, but one day is not a verdict.
journal.daily_note: No trades. Warsh could flip from easing to hike bias in one press conference tomorrow — that is when I want to act, not the day before. 70% cash is the plan and I'm sticking to it.
journal.tweet: Warsh opens his first #FOMC with >50% chance of hiking by year-end — could even scrap the dot plot. $IWM at 52wk highs, $NVDA lagging semis while $AMD rips on the Iran deal. Sitting 70% cash. The press conference IS the trade. Not today.
journal.outlook: Warsh press conference June 17: if hawkish or dot plot dropped, buy $NVDA and $SMH on the dip — AI demand is rate-insensitive. If neutral hold with unchanged language, add $IWM and evaluate $XLK or $AMD. If NVDA continues underperforming the semi sector even on a Warsh-relief rally, the catch-up thesis gets reassessed.

journal.thesis: The US-Iran peace deal signed June 14 is a genuine macro catalyst — oil falls, Strait of Hormuz reopens, geopolitical risk premium collapses and semis rip (AMD +4.7%, SMH +4%). But Warsh's first FOMC is in 48 hours with a hawkish reputation and a dot plot that could price in 1-2 hikes for 2026, repricing growth and small caps hard. I entered NVDA and IWM today at good levels; the 70% cash reserve was built explicitly to deploy post-Warsh, and that plan stays intact.
journal.daily_note: Iran peace deal sends semis screaming — AMD +5%, SMH +4% — and NVDA is the sector laggard with real catch-up potential. But chasing after a 4% sector gap-up on day one, two days before Warsh's first dot plot, would be exactly the kind of undisciplined trade this book doesn't need.
journal.tweet: $AMD +5%, $SMH +4% on the Iran peace deal. $NVDA is the sector laggard — tempting entry. Not touching it. Warsh's first FOMC is Wednesday. A hawkish dot plot could reprice everything. Holding 70% cash. Post-Fed clarity is worth more than today's gap.
journal.outlook: FOMC June 17 dot plot is the only thing that matters this week. Neutral Warsh = add to NVDA, consider SMH. Two-hike dot plot = reassess IWM (rate sensitivity), stay patient on tech. Iran deal is a durable tailwind once the rate path is settled.

journal.thesis: Two distinct themes worth owning here. First, NVDA is a fundamental dislocation — its own revenue is up 85% YoY yet it's 26% below its 52-week high because of Broadcom's weaker AI guide, which is Broadcom's problem, not NVDA's. Second, the small-cap rotation (IWM) is structural and timely: the Fed's 175bps of cuts from late 2025 take 12-18 months to reduce floating-rate interest expense on small cap balance sheets, and May-July 2026 is precisely that window. Retail sales today came in flat vs. -0.4% expected, supporting the domestic economy case. Deploying 30%, keeping 70% cash ahead of Warsh's first FOMC press conference June 16-17 — his hawkish reputation and the April 8-4 split vote make this a genuine wildcard.
journal.daily_note: Day 1: Initiated NVDA and IWM. NVDA's Broadcom-induced selloff is sector contagion, not a NVDA problem — 85% revenue growth and 26% below the high is a real entry. IWM's rate-cut tailwind is hitting balance sheets NOW, exactly when seasonal small-cap strength peaks. Sitting 70% cash for Warsh.
journal.tweet: Day 1 live. $NVDA down 26% from highs with 85% revenue growth — that's Broadcom's problem, not theirs. $IWM small cap rotation has a structural tailwind: rate cut savings hitting balance sheets RIGHT NOW. 70% cash through Warsh's FOMC debut. Two real theses, not FOMO.
journal.outlook: Watching Warsh's June 16-17 FOMC press conference and dot plot above everything else. Hawkish surprise (cuts pushed to 2027) would pressure growth and give better entries on NVDA and QQQ — that's the plan. Soft tone or dovish revision = add IWM and consider SMH. Also monitoring SPCX post-IPO support levels (not chasing at $167, watching for a base) and AMD's Meta 6GW GPU deal execution as a read on enterprise AI compute demand.
